one_line_takeaway: GCG jailbreaks scale (with degraded but nonzero success) to a 20B-parameter model, but reported attack-success rates are highly sensitive to evaluation protocol -- prefix-heuristic scoring overstates success versus GPT-4o semantic judgment -- and reasoning/coding prompts are more vulnerable than safety-benchmark prompts at every scale tested.
key_finding: (1) Attack success rate falls sharply as model size grows: on AdvBench under prefix-based scoring, ASR was 93.3% (Qwen2.5-0.5B) vs 68.7% (LLaMA-3.2-1B); under GPT-4o judgment, 62.6% (Qwen2.5-0.5B), 39.9% (LLaMA-3.2-1B), and only 4.0% (GPT-OSS-20B) -- the paper frames the successful (if low-rate) attack on a 20B model as the first demonstration that direct GCG scales to that size. (2) Prefix-based heuristics substantially overestimate success versus GPT-4o judgment across every model/dataset (e.g., Qwen2.5-0.5B: 93.3% prefix vs 62.6% GPT-4o on AdvBench; prefix scoring collapses almost to uselessness on GPT-OSS-20B because its 'thinking' responses rarely contain literal refusal strings, while GPT-4o still flags 4.0% as harmful). (3) Coding-generation prompts are more attackable than AdvBench safety prompts at every model scale: LLaMA-3.2-1B ASR rises from 68.7%→80.6% (prefix) and 39.9%→63.4% (GPT-4o) on coding vs AdvBench; GPT-OSS-20B GPT-4o-judged ASR rises from 4.0% (AdvBench) to 12.32% (coding). (4) T-GCG (annealed) on LLaMA-3.2-1B/AdvBench reached ASR_prefix=73.6% at alpha=0.005 (vs 68.7% for vanilla GCG prefix score), but GPT-4o-judged ASR (37.8%) was statistically indistinguishable from vanilla GCG's 39.9% given the reported standard deviations; increasing alpha to 0.01 lowered both prefix ASR (66.9%) and GPT-4o ASR (31.8%), indicating too much exploration dilutes the optimization signal. The authors describe T-GCG's benefits as 'preliminary' and 'modest.'
core_contribution: An empirical appraisal of gradient-based GCG (Greedy Coordinate Gradient) jailbreak attacks on aligned LLMs, extending evaluation to a much larger model (GPT-OSS-20B) than prior GCG work (previously tested mostly on 3B-13B models), and directly comparing prefix-based refusal-string heuristics against GPT-4o semantic judgments of attack success. The paper also proposes T-GCG, a simulated-annealing variant of GCG that replaces GCG's deterministic top-k token selection and loss-minimizing suffix acceptance with temperature-weighted stochastic sampling at both stages (candidate token probability P(x_j) = exp(-g_j/T1)/sum, suffix acceptance P(s_b) = exp(-Δl_b/T2)/sum), intended to escape local minima that trap vanilla GCG's greedy search.
threat_model: White-box gradient-based jailbreak attack: the attacker has full access to model weights/gradients and appends an optimized adversarial suffix to a harmful instruction prompt, optimizing (via the GCG coordinate-gradient procedure or its T-GCG annealed variant) to maximize the likelihood of an affirmative/compliant completion (e.g., 'Sure, ...') instead of a refusal. Two prompt domains are attacked: (1) AdvBench-style adversarial safety prompts requesting harmful actions, and (2) a set of GPT-4o-generated harmful coding-generation prompts (e.g., requests to write scripts that delete audit logs, exploit vulnerabilities, etc.).
limitations: T-GCG results are explicitly labeled preliminary and tested on only one model (LLaMA-3.2-1B) and one dataset (AdvBench), with only two alpha values; the paper itself states results are inconclusive under GPT-4o judgment ('the ASR values more or less the same, within the error bar'). No transferability experiments across models/families are reported (paper lists this as future work). The 'harmful coding-generation prompts' dataset and the GPT-4o judge template were both authored/generated by GPT-4o itself, so both the harmful test prompts and part of the evaluation pipeline share provenance in one LLM, a potential circularity/bias not addressed. Small batch size (B=100) and only 200 epochs were used for computational feasibility, which the authors acknowledge may understate GCG's true attack potential. Only one white-box attack family (GCG/T-GCG) is studied; no comparison to other jailbreak method classes (template-based, hybrid) is run empirically, only cited.

datasets_used: GPT-4o-generated harmful coding-generation prompt set (100 prompts, described in Appendix A)
quantitative_results: AdvBench ASR_prefix: Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct 93.3% (std 2.1), LLaMA-3.2-1B-Instruct 68.7% (std 5.1), GPT-OSS-20B not reported (prefix heuristic fails)
Coding prompts ASR_gpt-4o: Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct 66.1% (std 4.4), LLaMA-3.2-1B-Instruct 63.4% (std 3.9), GPT-OSS-20B 12.32% (std 4.15)
T-GCG on LLaMA-3.2-1B-Instruct, AdvBench: alpha=0.005 -> ASR_prefix 73.6% (std 4.8), ASR_gpt-4o 37.8% (std 5.3); alpha=0.01 -> ASR_prefix 66.9% (std 3.6), ASR_gpt-4o 31.8% (std 3.8)
baselines_compared: Vanilla GCG (Zou et al. 2023, direct coordinate-gradient suffix optimization) is the primary baseline against which T-GCG is compared
Prefix-based refusal-string heuristic vs GPT-4o semantic judgment are compared as two competing evaluation protocols for the same attacks (not separate attack methods)
